statusLine: WTI crude trades at elevated $104.54/bbl amid Middle East supply route disruptions, while US petroleum inventories decline sharply across all major products.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets remain under pressure with WTI at $104.54/bbl, down just 1.07% despite significant geopolitical risks. The unusual $11.77 premium of WTI over Brent reflects tight North American supply conditions and Middle East shipping disruptions that have redirected global flows. US production holds steady at 13.6 million bpd, but commercial crude inventories dropped by 4.14 million barrels to 409.2 million barrels, indicating strong domestic demand draw.

Petroleum product markets show acute tightness with gasoline stocks falling 6.33 million barrels and distillates declining 3.12 million barrels in the latest EIA report. Despite these inventory draws, retail prices have retreated with gasoline down 7.6 cents to $4.18/gallon and diesel falling 20.5 cents to $5.40/gallon, suggesting recent price spikes may have begun to moderate demand. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains at 77.9 million barrels, well below historical norms.

Critical maritime chokepoints face severe disruption with Houthi attacks targeting Red Sea shipping lanes and Iranian threats in the Strait of Hormuz. These advisories directly impact global crude flows, with tankers avoiding the Suez Canal route and lengthening voyage times to Americas markets. The closure of Hormuz mentioned in CREA research represents a major supply shock, though renewable energy growth has partially offset demand impacts on global fossil fuel consumption.

Americas producers face mixed conditions with US shale maintaining robust output levels while pipeline constraints continue limiting Canadian oil sands access to markets. The WTI-Brent spread suggests North American crude is commanding premium pricing due to supply security advantages over Middle East grades facing shipping risks. Guyana's Stabroek block and Brazil's pre-salt continue ramping production, providing some supply diversity for regional markets.
keyPoints: WTI crude at $104.54/bbl trades at unusual $11.77 premium over Brent due to Middle East shipping disruptions
US petroleum inventories fell sharply with crude down 4.14 million barrels and gasoline dropping 6.33 million barrels
Critical maritime advisories show Houthi attacks in Red Sea and Iranian threats in Strait of Hormuz disrupting global oil flows
US production steady at 13.6 million bpd while retail fuel prices declined despite inventory draws
Strategic Petroleum Reserve at 77.9 million barrels remains well below historical levels
Americas crude markets benefit from supply security premium as Middle East shipping routes face sustained attacks
